segments.text: All right.
Good morning. Morning. Morning,
Everyone. Welcome along
to the committee.
Today, we are looking forward
to today's hearing on the defense Major Projects report
Um, we have half an hour sit down
for this morning's discussion.
I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions from members,
but we'd like to hand over to you both for initial comments
and then we can delve into it. Thank you.
Thank you, um, for the opportunity
to attend this oral hearing today
for the defense Major projects Report 2024.
Uh, my name is Sarah Minson.
I'm the Defense Secretary Capability Delivery at
the Ministry of Defense.
And with me today is the Defense Force Assistant Chief
Capability, Bri Matt.
We, this is the 15th edition of the Major Projects Report.
The series was established to provide greater visibility
and scrutiny of major defense acquisition projects.
And in the report you will see
that we are reporting on projects across four domains.
So there's 12 projects across four domains.
Maritime, land, air, and information delivering new
or upgraded capability remains vital to ensuring
that defense can continue to deliver safe
and effective safely and effectively to the level
that the government requires, and that our allies
and partners have come to expect.
The ongoing publication of this report
demonstrates a commitment to transparency
and assurance about defense managed projects.
I'd like to acknowledge the work of Audit New Zealand,
who have provided independent, uh, reviews
of these reports since 2019.
The projects in this report are part of an overall portfolio
of around 6 billion worth
of ministry led capability projects.
And this report focuses on the medium
to high risk projects that we are delivering.
It includes $2.4 billion acquisition
of the P eight Poseidon fleet
and the associated training materials and systems
and infrastructure, as well as the 1.5 billion replacement
of the Hercules fleet and associated infrastructure
and training systems.
Both of those large projects were delivered ahead
of schedule and under budget.
And last week we were proud to celebrate the formal opening
of the home of the p eights and number five squadron.
At IP alongside current projects, we are focused on
what is required to deliver projects within the 2025
defense capability plan.
The plan commitments of 12 billion over the next four
years is significant.
We must deliver on time within budget
and within scope, providing value for money,
but also providing innovative thinking.
The operating model we use to deliver project delivery.
The cap, the Defense Capability Management system is a
proven model that is scalable and resilient.
Clearly, our industry partners are a key part
of successful delivery, and we look forward
to reporting in future on the defense industry strategy
that is in development and will
be released in the next few months.
I'll now pass on to Brigette Western for smoking comments.
Koto, it's a pleasure to be here with you today
to discuss the, uh, major defense capability projects.
The world remains an unstable
and dangerous place, one
where the rules based international order
that we rely on New Zealand is constantly under threat.
This topic has been well covered in previous committees,
so I won't dwell on it.
The major projects
and indeed the Defense Capability Plan are designed
to improve the New Zealand Defense Force's ability
to understand partner
and then act in response to the New Zealand security needs.
We in the N-Z-N-Z-D-F understand investments
and military capability often come at the
cost of other priorities.
We are well aware that it was our responsibility
to turn major projects into productive military capabilities
that serve New Zealand's needs.
This requires the integration of input such
as infrastructure, workforce, information systems, and data,
and key aspects of interop interoperability such
as training and doctrine.
In doing so, we must continue
to be rigorous in managing cost pressures and budget.
Due to the current global security situation,
there is an imperative to deliver military capabilities
whilst continuing
to improve the New Zealand Defense Forces Preparedness
and Readiness to operate.
Now, the NZD has maintaining its commitment
to the rules based international order
for ongoing operations
to support the Ukraine in the Middle East and in North Asia.
The, at the same time, we are fielding new capabilities
as we improve our interoperability
and demonstrate intent to act within the Pacific
to both our partners and to potential malign actors.
Recent examples include Thar's current deployment
to the Middle East and the Indo-Pacific,
and she returns with her crew this Sunday
after seven months, deployed a significant deployment
to excise Talisman Saber,
which include new network enabled Army capabilities
and our newly acquired Bushmaster vehicles.
The deployment of UNC crewed maritime systems
to provide maritime domain awareness in Fiji,
and that is ongoing as we speak.
And the recent use of p eights in North Asia
and the southwest Pacific for maritime domain awareness
and our new C one 30 js for meeting
operations in the Antarctica.
That was well covered then.
NZDF is operating at a high tempo, has work
to understand partner and then act while,
while still say developing
and delivering new capabilities guided
by the defense capability plan.
Our people are making great progress,
building their combat capability
and a avoid by the government's commitment
to invest in their ability
to defend New Zealand and our interests.
We continue to operate with courage, commitment,
comradeship, and integrity.
